title: Orthogonal Subspace Decomposition for Generalizable AI-Generated Image Detection
abstract: Detecting AI-generated images (AIGIs), such as natural images or face images, has become increasingly important yet challenging. In this paper, we start from a new perspective to excavate the reason behind the failure generalization in AIGI detection, named the asymmetry phenomenon, where a naively trained detector tends to favor overfitting to the limited and monotonous fake patterns, causing the feature space to become highly constrained and low-ranked, which is proved seriously limiting the expressivity and generalization. One potential remedy is incorporating the pre-trained knowledge within the vision foundation models (higher-ranked) to expand the feature space, alleviating the model’s overfitting to fake. To this end, we employ Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to decompose the original feature space into two orthogonal subspaces. By freezing the principal components and adapting only the remained components, we preserve the pre-trained knowledge while learning fake patterns. Compared to existing full-parameters and LoRA-based tuning methods, we explicitly ensure orthogonality, enabling the higher rank of the whole feature space, effectively minimizing overfitting and enhancing generalization. We finally identify a crucial insight: our method implicitly learns a vital prior that fakes are actually derived from the real, indicating a hierarchical relationship rather than independence. Modeling this prior, we believe, is essential for achieving superior generalization. Our codes are publicly available at GitHub.
impact: This research advances application-driven machine learning by proposing a novel method for detecting AI-generated images. By effectively identifying deepfakes and curbing malicious uses of generative models, it holds significant potential for positive social impact. However, a possible negative outcome is the misuse of our method to enhance the realism of deepfake generators. To address this concern, we plan to implement measures like access control. Overall, we urge the research community to minimize negative impacts while leveraging the positive contributions of this work. Acknowledgment This work was supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62202014, 62332002, 62425101).

query_input.scenario: Video authentication for detecting lip-syncing fraud
query_input.issue: Detector misses subtle temporal forgery clues, failing to flag fraudulent lip-synced videos.
hazard: Overfitting to synthetic forgery patterns via LoRA fine-tuning on limited data degrades cross-domain deepfake detection generalization.
exposure: Legal and media professionals using detection to verify video evidence.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Institutional reliance on automated detectors without complementary human expertise to verify subtle forgery clues independently.
hipporag_result.impact: Fraudulent lip-synced videos admitted as authentic evidence cause wrongful legal decisions and widespread media misinformation.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Overfitting to synthetic forgery patterns via LoRA fine-tuning degrades deepfake detection, through legal and media professionals using detection, triggers institutional reliance on automated detectors without human expertise, leading to fraudulent lip-synced videos causing wrongful legal decisions and media misinformation.

dose_response: Overfitting to synthetic forgery patterns causes lip-sync detectors to miss temporal anomalies, leading legal professionals to accept fraudulent videos as genuine, compromising legal integrity.
vulnerability: Lip-sync detectors insensitive to temporal anomalies fail to flag manipulated videos, creating false authenticity.
impact: Fraudulent lip-synced videos accepted as genuine evidence, compromising legal and media integrity.
